coredata.dc:title: Highly sensitive B, N co-doped carbon dots for fluorescent and colorimetric dual-mode detection of mercury ions in wastewater
coredata.dc:creator: Fu, Qing
Long, Caicheng
Huang, Jiaoyan
Liu, Sicong
Qing, Taiping
Zhang, Peng
Feng, Bo
coredata.dc:description: Mercury ions (Hg2+) owing to excessive discharge cause serious damage to human health and the water ecosystem via bioaccumulation in food chains. Novel materials employed for the high-performance monitoring of Hg2+ are urgently required. Herein, using sucrose, boric acid, and melamine as raw materials, a new nanomaterial sensor, namely boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (B, N-CDs), was designed for fluorescent and colorimetric dual-mode detection of Hg2+. Because of the occurrence of both dynamic and static quenching, B, N-CDs were utilized to function as fluorescent sensors for detecting Hg2+ with a limit of detection (LOD) of 5.3 nM. B, N-CDs performed an action for catalytic oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to a blue cationic radical via peroxidase mimetic activity. By sequentially adding cysteine and Hg2+ to control the emergence of the TMB cation radical, a sensor for the Hg2+ assay was established through the colorimetric "on-off-on" signal and the LOD was as low as 7.8 nM. Moreover, the application potential of B, N-CDs for complex water environments was demonstrated to be excellent. In summary, the dual-mode detection method delivers some valid strategies for the detection of mercury in aqueous solutions employing the functional nanomaterials mentioned, and opens new avenues in tackling the problem of heavy metal ion pollution for environmental monitoring and remediation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanomaterial sensor
Mercury ion detection
B, N-CDs
Fluorescent and colorimetric

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Calculation of the fluorescence quantum yield
Detection of mercury ions with the B, N-CDs
Chosen parameters to evaluate the analytical method
Establishment of the dual-mode sensor for Hg2+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: High-resolution transmission electron microscopy spectra (a), size distribution histograms (b), Fourier transform infrared spectrometer spectra (c), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey spectra (d), N 1 s spectra (e), and B 1 s spectra (f) of the B, N-CDs.

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrum, the excitation and emission spectra (λem (emission wavelength) = 365 nm, and λex (excitation wavelength) = 300 nm). Inset: Images of B, N-CDs under the sunlight (left) and 365 nm UV lamp (right) (a); 3D fluorescence spectrum (b); fluorescence (FL) spectra at the different excitation wavelength. (inset: normalized intensity of different excitation wavelengths.) (c); FL intensity variation at different pH (d); 7 d (e); and 2 h (f) of the synthesized B, N-CDs.

Fluorescence intensity (FL) emission spectra of B, N-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of Hg2+ (0–60 μM) (a); Curve of F/F0 and Hg2+ content, where F0 and F represent FL values of B, N-CDs at 365 nm after and before the addition of Hg2+, respectively (b); Fluorescence quenching rate (F/F0) of B, N-CDs versus diverse metal ions (30 μM) (Ex =300 nm, Em=365 nm) (c); Absorbance spectra of the B, N-CDs after the introduction of different contents of Hg2+ (0–60 μM) (d); Curve of absorbance and Hg2+ content (e); Absorbance of B, N-CDs versus diverse metal ions (0.6 μM) (537 nm) (f).

Absorbance of H2O2 mediated oxidation of TMB forB, N-CDs, B, N-CDs/Cys, and B, N-CDs/Cys/Hg2+ systems. The contents of B, N-CDs, Cys, and Hg2+ are 80 μg/mL, 20 μM, and 10 μM, respectively (a); Fluorescence intensity (FL) decay traces of B, N-CDs before (black curve) and after (red curve) the addition of Hg2+ (b); FL intensity response (F0/F) of the sensor versus Hg2+ content, where F0 and F are FL values of the sensor (365 nm) before and after the addition of Hg2+, respectively (c). Zeta-potential in the absence and presence of Hg2+ (d); FTIR spectrum in the absence and presence of Hg2+ (e); N 1 s before and after the addition of Hg2+ (f) of B, N-CDs.
Schematic diagram of fluorescence quenching mechanism (a), and the colorimetric mechanism (b).
Schematic representation of the synthesis of boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (B, N-CDs) (a), and the performance of mercury ion detection through fluorescence/ colorimetry (b).
Recovery rates of Hg2+ in real water samples by dual-mode detection.

head.ce:title: Highly sensitive B, N co-doped carbon dots for fluorescent and colorimetric dual-mode detection of mercury ions in wastewater

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environment and Resources, Xiangtan University, Xiangtan 411105, Hunan Province, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environment and Resources, Xiangtan University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Environment and Resources, Xiangtan University, Xiangtan 411105, Hunan Province, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mercury ions (Hg2+) owing to excessive discharge cause serious damage to human health and the water ecosystem via bioaccumulation in food chains. Novel materials employed for the high-performance monitoring of Hg2+ are urgently required. Herein, using sucrose, boric acid, and melamine as raw materials, a new nanomaterial sensor, namely boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (B, N-CDs), was designed for fluorescent and colorimetric dual-mode detection of Hg2+. Because of the occurrence of both dynamic and static quenching, B, N-CDs were utilized to function as fluorescent sensors for detecting Hg2+ with a limit of detection (LOD) of 5.3 nM. B, N-CDs performed an action for catalytic oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to a blue cationic radical via peroxidase mimetic activity. By sequentially adding cysteine and Hg2+ to control the emergence of the TMB cation radical, a sensor for the Hg2+ assay was established through the colorimetric "on-off-on" signal and the LOD was as low as 7.8 nM. Moreover, the application potential of B, N-CDs for complex water environments was demonstrated to be excellent. In summary, the dual-mode detection method delivers some valid strategies for the detection of mercury in aqueous solutions employing the functional nanomaterials mentioned, and opens new avenues in tackling the problem of heavy metal ion pollution for environmental monitoring and remediation.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal pollution in water has remained a serious challenge in the field of environmental pollution control because of its harmful effects on human organisms and ecosystems [1]. Among heavy metal ions, mercury ions (Hg2+) are considered the most common and noxious transition metal ions globally. Mercury-containing wastewater is discharged from industries such as Chlor-alkali, plastics, batteries, and electronics. When the content of mercury reaches one part per million (ppm), it will pose a threat to environmental and ecological issues under such a low-concentration background environment [2–4]. Meanwhile, a large number of residues remain in surface water and groundwater, and accumulate in organisms and human bodies through the food chain because mercury is not biodegradable. The main reason for health hazards is the ingestion of aquatic animals and water holding excessive mercury, such as Minamata disease in Japan [5,6]. Therefore, effectively tracking and monitoring the mercury content in the environment and effectively controlling pollution sources that cause harm to human beings is of major concern to humans.

The classical detection of Hg2+ is mainly based on electrochemical analysis, atomic fluorescence method, mass spectrometry, but the need for expert handling, excessive cost, and time consumption, which significantly limit their practical applications [7,8]. With the continuous development of nanotechnology in recent years, many researchers have made significant efforts to develop fluorescent nanomaterials for sensing Hg2+ such as metal-organic frameworks, metal nanoparticles, and carbon dots (CDs) [9–11]. The mechanism of these materials is mostly to change the intrinsic fluorescence intensity as the output signal. Although these materials are effective in detecting Hg2+, large background signal interference and fluctuations in instrument conditions remain a challenge. In recent years, the development of a dual-mode system for sensing environmental pollutants has attracted considerable research interest because the dual-channel assay mode can remove the fluctuation in fluorescence intensity caused by external environmental factors [12–16]. For example, Liu et al. [17] utilized CDs for a colorimetry and fluorescence assay of Fe3+. Compared with traditional single-mode detection, the dual-mode not only exhibits high-performance fluorescence detection but also directly monitors the substance through color changes observable to the naked eye. Although many studies have described the application of these modes for biosensing and immunoassays, very few reports have been published on the fluorescent and colorimetric detection of Hg2+ in actual water samples.

CDs have been certificated to possess peroxidase activity, easy to functionalize, low toxicity, and low cost, which are highly desirable nanomaterials for simple monitoring of mercury levels [18,19]. In this study, boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (B, N-CDs) with excellent fluorescent and peroxidase mimetic properties were fabricated from boric acid, melamine, and sucrose using a one-pot hydrothermal synthesis method. Herein, we demonstrate a fluorescent and colorimetric dual-mode system for the simple and rapid monitoring of Hg2+ in actual water samples. The rationale for this strategy is based on the fact that the fluorescence intensity of B, N-CDs is significantly quenched in the presence of Hg2+ through a combination of static and dynamic quenching. After the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) by B, N-CDs, a blue-colored cation radical was generated. Cysteine (Cys) and Hg2+ were employed to control cation radical generation, which endows B, N-CDs with the ability of colorimetric sensing of Hg2+. The dual-signal platform for Hg2+ monitoring can not only achieve high-sensitivity and high-selectivity fluorescent detection, but also facilitate intuitive naked eye observation through obvious color changes. Overall, B, N-CDs are desirable to be a new dual-mode function platform for high-performance and visible detection of Hg2+ in wastewater.

Sucrose, 30% hydrogen peroxide, boric acid, dimethyl sulfoxide, ethanol, melamine, cysteine (Cys), and TMB were purchased from Shanghai Jinsui Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Hg(NO3)2 and other metal ions were obtained from Shanghai Songon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water was used as the solvent.

A transmission electron microscope was used to image the morphology of B, N-CDs (Tecnai G2 F20, USA). A Fourier transform infrared spectrometer was used to characterize the chemical structure (FTIR, Nicolet iS10, UK). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was utilized to explore chemical elements (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, US). The fluorescence lifetime decay curve was obtained using steady-state/transient spectrometers (FLS 980, Edinburgh). All absorption spectra were obtained via an ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The spectrophotometer equipped with a 5/2.5 slit width was used to measure the fluorescent spectra (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan).

Sucrose (1 g), boric acid (0.5 g), and melamine (1.0 g) were added into 50 mL ultrapure water by ultrasonic treatment for 10 min. Then, the mixed solution was burned in a drying oven at 180 °C for 6 h to obtain the CDs solution. Next, the solution was filtered by a filter membrane (0.22 μM). The 1000 Da dialysis membrane was utilized to dialyze the filtered solution for 3 days. Finally, the dialyzed solution was freeze-dried into a solid and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of the fluorescence quantum yield
ce:section.ce:para:
Quinine sulfate was selected to calculate the quantum yield (QY) of the B, N-CDs as a reference substance. The absorbance and fluorescence intensity of quinine sulfate were measured (QY = 54%, Excitation wavelength = 300 nm). The QY was obtained using the following equation [20–22].
(1)
Y
X
=
Y
S
F
X
A
S
η
X
2
F
S
A
X
η
S
2
Where FL intensity, absorbance, and refractive index are represented by F, A, and η, respectively. The "s" and "x" represent known reference substances and substances to be tested, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of mercury ions with the B, N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The capability of B, N-CDs for monitoring Hg2+ in the dual-mode fluorescent and colorimetric system was evaluated. The fluorescence changes of the prepared materials were measured using an F-7000 spectrophotometer after the introduction of Hg2+. Regarding colorimetric detection, B, N-CDs were used to mimic peroxidase enzyme activity to catalyze TMB color development, and the changes in absorbance were observed at 657 nm. Then, the change in colorimetric signal with the introduction of Cys and Hg2+ to the above system was observed at 657 nm. To explore the linear relationship and minimum detection concentration of the dual-mode system, the fluorescent and colorimetric signals were recorded after the addition of different concentrations of Hg2+ to B, N-CDs. In addition, the selectivity capacity of the sensor was investigated. Common metal ions and various anions were presented separately as interfering substances, and the influence of their content on Hg2+ detection was evaluated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chosen parameters to evaluate the analytical method
ce:section.ce:para:
The parameters of selectivity, precision, linearity range [limit of detection (LOD), limit of quantification (LOQ)], repeatability, intermediate precision, and robustness were used to evaluate the analytical method. The fixed parameters in the evaluation of analytical methods were shown in Tables S1 and S2. The analytical curve was derived from average triplicate measurements. LOD and LOQ were calculated according to IUPAC definitions by Eqs. (2) and (3).
(2)
LOD = 3 *
σ
K
(3)
LOQ = 10 *
σ
K
Where σ is the standard deviation of the blank, K is the slope of the analytical curve (analytical sensitivity) [23].

The precision was assessed by repeatability and intermediate precision composition. Repeatability was performed by two concentrations (1.00 ×10−4 and 2.00 ×10−4 mol/ L−1) and three different periods (16, 20, and 24 h) for ten repeatability tests, using a fluorescence spectrophotometer and an UV–vis spectrophotometer monitoring. Also, the intermediate precision (or repeatability) test was carried out by two different analysts on a single cycle (16, 20, and 24 h) and a single concentration (1.00 ×10−4 and 2.00 ×10−4 mol/ L−1) of the analyte. Repeatability testing requires consistency of all other parameters during fluorescence and colorimetric experiments. "RSD" [Eq. (4)] and "Hor. Rat-ratio" (=RSD/RSD Horwitz) [Eq. (5)] can be found through experiments and calculations. The expected "Hor. Rat-ratio" value must be less than 1 [24].
(4)
RSD
%
=
s
x
¯
(5)
RSD Horwitz
%
= 0.67 ×
2
1 − 0.5 log
C
Where s is the standard deviation,
x
¯
is the average value of fluorescence intensity or absorbance, C is the concentration.

To evaluate the practical application of the dual-mode detection of Hg2+ in a complicated water environment, two water samples were collected, one from the Xiangtan electroplating plant and the other from the Xiangjiang River, and tested. The actual sample recovery rate is measured by the method of standard addition. A series of pre-treatments were performed in two kinds of water samples to make the water samples free of Hg2+, and then quantitative Hg2+ were added to the water samples, and the ratio of the obtained results to the theoretical value is the recovery rate. The following formula was utilized to evaluate the practical application of wastewater [Eqs. (7) and (8)]. Each sample was measured five times.
(7)
P =
C
2
C
1
× 100 %
(8)
RSD =
standard
deviation
x
¯
Where P is the Hg2+ recovery rate, C
1 is the standard theoretical concentrations of Hg2+ added to these water samples, and C
2 is the detected Hg2+ content in water samples, respectively. The critical T-test was used to assess the recovery rate, and the calculation formula is shown in Eq. (9).
(9)
T =
w − η
n
s
Where w is the average value of the samples, η is the true value, n is the number of samples, and s is the standard deviation. The critical values of T at different degrees of freedom (n-1) can be checked by the critical distribution table. The calculated T value shouldn’t exceed the critical value, indicating that the system has little error.

The sensor was obtained following the hydrothermal synthesis of boric acid, melamine, and sucrose ( Scheme 1). Moreover, the optimization conditions, including B/N molar ratios, the amount of sucrose, reaction temperature, and hydrothermal time were modified to explore their effects on the QY and peroxidase catalase activity of the prepared sensor. These QYs of the synthesized sensor were calculated in Fig. S1 [25]. The sensor mediated catalytic oxidation of TMB caused an obvious colorimetric reaction, and the absorbance was measured (Fig. S2). In summary, the synthesis conditions for achieving optimal QYs and catalase activity were as follows: B/N ratio = 1:3, sucrose = 1 g, temperature = 180 °C, and time = 6 h.

To explore the elemental composition and functional groups of the sensor, the nanoscale morphology of the sensor was spherical in shape and the lattice fringe distance was 0.28 nm ( Fig. 1a) [17]. The particle size distribution ranged from 3.0 to 6.2 nm. The average diameter was about 4.5 nm (Fig. 1b) [26]. The chemical properties and chemical bonds of the sensor were confirmed through FTIR and XPS spectroscopy. The vibrational absorption bonds of O−H were measured at 3425 cm−1, these vibration absorption bonds of CO and C−N resulted in peaks at 1633 cm−1 and 1402 cm−1 (Fig. 1c) [27,28]. Moreover, these stretching vibrations of the B-OH and C-B bonds were distributed at 1205 cm−1 and 1075 cm−1 [29,30]. The results of FTIR analysis could therefore be employed to successfully demonstrate the doping of boron and nitrogen in the prepared material, offering a foundation for monitoring Hg2+.

The elemental composition of the synthesized nanosensor was shown in Fig. 1d. Four different characteristic peaks were observed in the XPS spectra at 191.61 eV, 284.65 eV, 531.99 eV, and 399.47 eV, attributed to boron (B 1 s), nitrogen (N 1 s), oxygen (O 1 s), and carbon (C 1 s), respectively. Fig. 1e revealed the existence of C−N − C (398.8 eV), N − H (399.6 eV), N − (C)3 (400.0 eV), and N − N (400.8 eV) peaks in the B, N-CDs [31]. Besides, Fig. 1f further indicated that the peaks at 190.8, 192.1, and 193.4 eV were originated from C−B, B2O3, and H3BO3, respectively [32,33]. The sensor was successfully prepared through the one-pot hydrothermal treatment employing boric acid and melamine as boron and nitrogen sources, respectively, as confirmed by XPS analysis. Therefore, the XPS analysis confirmed that B, N-CDs are rich in hydrophilic groups, endowing B, N-CDs with good dispersibility in solution and significant potential for sensing metal ions. These results were consistent with those of the FTIR analysis [2].

Fluorescence and absorption figures were obtained to indagate these optical characteristics of the sensor. Fig. 2a indicates that the typical peak at 281 nm is originated from the π-π * transition of the aromatic sp2 domains in the UV–vis spectrum [34]. Moreover, as shown in the inset of Fig. 2a, B, N-CDs were observed to emit an obvious blue fluorescence. From Fig. 2a, it was inferred that the peaks at 300 nm and 365 nm were the best excitation and best emission position of B, N-CDs, respectively. The 3D fluorescence scan shown in Fig. 2b also shows one obvious excitation peak and emission peak (300/365 nm). Fig. 2c illustrates that the increasing of excitation positions (280–450 nm), and these emission peaks of B, N-CDs were red-shifted. The surface chemical properties and size distribution resulted in this sensor performing the excitation-dependent behavior [35,36]. In addition, different pH conditions were used to explore the fluorescence stability of the synthetic nanosensor. Fig. 3d shows that there was no obvious change in the fluorescence intensity with change in pH (1−12). As shown in Fig. 3e and f, fluorescence intensity fluctuations scarcely appeared with time. Therefore, B, N-CDs have strong fluorescence stability, excellent optical properties, and provide a platform for practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of the dual-mode sensor for Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: The dual-mode sensing performance of the sensor with Hg2+ was examined. In the fluorescence detection mode, Hg2+ performs as a quencher, which can effectively reduce the fluorescence of the sensor. The optimization of the fluorescence reaction conditions was firstly investigated (Fig. S4a,b,c, and d). The optimized conditions were as follows: the concentration of the sensor, 80 μg/mL; temperature, 20 °C; pH, 9.0, (phosphate buffer solution); time, 2 min. From Fig. 3a, the fluorescence values of B, N-CDs had a decreasing change as the content of Hg2+ increased. As shown in Fig. 3b, the fitting curve of the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F/F0) versus these concentrations of Hg2+ had an excellent linear relationship (0–0.2 μM, R2 = 0.99126). (where the fluorescence values of the sensor before and after the addition of Hg2+ are represented by F and F0, respectively.) The LOD was 5.3 nM (3 s/k, where s is the standard deviation of the blank sample, k represents the slope of the calibration curve). To investigate the selectivity effect of the B, N-CDs on Hg2+, common interfering substances, including various metal cations (Zn2+, Ba2+, Al3+, Mg2+, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, and Li+), were investigated. As shown in Fig. 3c, only Hg2+ could completely reduce the fluorescence of the sensor. According to the previous studies, nitrogen-doped carbon dots have a fluorescence quenching effect on Cu2+ due to the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism [37,38]. Fe2+ had a marginal quenching impact on the fluorescence values of sensors. B, N-CDs showed little obvious fluorescence quenching influence on these metal ions, which further illustrates the specificity of our sensor for detection of Hg2+ (Fig. 3c). Besides, co-existing anions such as Br−, Cl−, F−, NO3 −, EDTA2−, CO3 2− and SO4 2−, which are commonly present in environmental and biological settings, also had little effect on the fluorescence intensity of the sensor (Fig. S9a).

The catalase activity of the sensor was confirmed by the oxidation of TMB. Catalysis by B, N-CDs resulted in a blue-colored product that had an obvious absorption peak at 657 nm (Fig. S5). Optimization of the catalytic activity was determined with different B, N-CD concentrations, hydrogen peroxide concentrations, reaction temperatures, pH of the solutions, and reaction times. As shown in Fig. S2b-f, these optimized conditions were determined as: B, N-CDs, 80 μg/mL; H2O2, 1 mM; temperature, 24 °C; pH, 3.9 (phosphate buffer solution); time, 15 min. Under the optimized conditions, changes in absorbance at 657 nm were observed using a UV spectrophotometer based on the TMB/H2O2 system with the addition of B, N-CDs, B, N-CDs/Cys, and B, N-CDs/Cys/Hg2+. As shown in Fig. 4a, after the addition of Cys, the colorimetric signal disappeared effectively, whereas the presence of Hg2+ caused the rapid restoration of the colorimetric signal. Inspired by this phenomenon, increasing the Hg2+ concentration resulted in a regular increase in absorbance. The absorbance had an excellent linear relationship versus the content of Hg2+ and LOD was 7.8 nM (0–0.3 μM, R2 = 0.99155). Owing to the complexity of the actual water samples, these studies of selectivity on the sensor were conducted. As shown in Fig. 3f, with the addition of different metal ions (same as above), all ions had almost no effect except for Cu2+, which could restore the colorimetric signal mainly because Cu2+ has a strong ability to catalyze TMB oxidation. Similarly, the co-existing anions such as Br−, Cl−, F−, NO3 −, EDTA2−, CO3 2− and SO4 2− were also explored, which had little influence on the catalase performance of the sensor (Fig. S9b).
From the analysis curve shown in Fig. 3b and e, LOD and LOQ were calculated to be 5.3 nM and 17.7 nM in fluorescence detection, and LOD and LOQ were 7.8 nM and 26 nM in colorimetric detection.

The validation results for the repeatability test were shown in Tables S8 and S9 at different levels (1 ×10−5 and 2 ×10−5 M) in three time periods (16 h, 20 h, and 24 h). These results indicated all the HorRat ratios are lower than 1. Besides, in order to compare the approximate value of Qcalculated with the value of Qcritical (Qcritical = 0.970), the Dixon test was performed on the results of each analysis period according to Eq. (10). The value of Qcalculated was smaller than Qcritical, indicating that the difference was not statistically significant (Table S7).
(10)
Q
calculated
=
x
n
¯
−
x
n
+
¯
1
¯
x
hightest
¯
−
x
lowest
¯
(11)
F
n = a ,
n = b
=
s
a
2
s
b
2

Then, the intermediate precision study was conducted by two different researchers. Three different cycle times and 1 × 10−5 or 2 × 10−5 M concentrations were employed for N = 10 repeated measurements (Tables S8 and S9). From the results obtained, all the HorRat ratios were calculated to be below 1. Fisher test (F-test) was calculated with Eq. (11). Since Fcalculated < Fcritical, the proposed method was accepted as reproducible. The Youden test is used to verify the robustness of the method and is expressed by Pareto’s graphs (Fig. S11). As illustrated in Fig. S11, the parameters (i) Storage temperature (parameter 1), (ii) pH (parameter 3) and (iii) temperature of analysis (parameter 7) were important, verifying the robustness of the proposed methodology. [39].

To discuss the mechanism of the sensor in the fluorescent and colorimetric monitoring of Hg2+, a series of experiments and calculations were performed. The fluorescence quenching mechanism is calculated using the Stern-Volmer formula [40,41]：
(12)
F
0
F
= 1 +
K
SV
∙ C
(13)
τ
=
Β
1
τ
1
2
+
B
2
τ
2
2
+
B
3
τ
3
2
B
1
τ
1
+
B
2
τ
2
+
B
3
τ
3
(14)
K
SV
=
K
q
∙
τ
0
where the FL signals before and after the addition of Hg2+ are represented by F0 and F, respectively, and C is Hg2+ content. K
SV represents the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, calculated as 0.36828 (Fig. 4c).
τ
0
is the fluorescence lifetime without the quencher. K
q represents the fluorescence quenching rate constant of 0.1083 × 109 (Fig. 4b).

A calculated Kq of less than Kdif (1.0 × 1010) indicates the occurrence of dynamic quenching. Subsequently, Hg2+ combines with certain functional groups of B, N-CDs and static quenching occurs [42,43]. As shown in Fig. 4d-f, the zeta-potential, FTIR, and N 1 s of the sensor were obtained. Zeta potential is highly negative in the presence of Hg2+ when the bond of –NH4 is connected with Hg2+. The positive potential was partly inhibited, indicating that Hg2+ mostly forms static coordination compounds with multiple –NH4, which was further confirmed by the FTIR and fitting high-resolution N 1 s. The fluorescence of this sensor is markedly quenched via the rapid energy transfer process and promoted interaction with Hg2+ ( Fig. 5a) [44]. First, the collision between Hg2+ and B, N-CDs produces dynamic quenching, and then, Hg2+ successfully binds to –NH4 of B, N-CDs to undergo static quenching [17,45].

The colorimetric mechanism aims to achieve the purpose of Hg2+ detection by inhibiting and restoring the generation of TMB-free radicals. As shown in Figs. 4a and 5b, in the presence of Cys, its thiol groups inhibited the generation of TMB-free radicals, which were reduced to the pristine colorless TMB substances through the process of hydrogen donation; thus, the blue color disappeared, and the absorbance at the peak position in the corresponding ultraviolet spectrum also decreased. After adding Hg2+, because of the strong binding force between Cys and Hg2+, the channel for generating TMB-free radicals was opened, the colorimetric signal was restored, and the absorbance value of the corresponding peak position was restored [46,47].

Monitoring Hg2+ in actual water samples was investigated. The wastewater from an electroplating plant and the Xiangjiang River were pretreated, and the actual water samples did not contain Hg2+. Various amounts of standard Hg2+ were introduced into these processed samples. These results calculated according to Eqs. (7) and (8) are shown in Table 1. The recovery rate was between 96% and 104%, which represents good recovery and high analytical precision. In order to better compare recovery rates of Hg2+ in real water samples, we also conducted recovery experiments with an Atomic fluorescence spectrometer (AFS-2202E) (Fig. S10). These results calculated according to Eqs. (7) and (8) are shown in Table 1. The recovery rate was between 92% and 106%. Due to the high requirements of operation technology and equipment conditions, the analytical instrument is relatively unstable in measuring recovery rate of Hg2+ at low concentrations. To a certain extent, the fluorescence and colorimetric dual-mode method is simple to operate and has a high sensitivity to the detection of Hg2+. Besides, the critical T-test was conducted to compare whether recovery rates is statistically different from 100% in the experiment. All the results were calculated in Table S10 according to Eq. (9). The results showed that the calculated T values are all less than the t critical value (2.776), indicating that there is little obvious systematic error in the recovery rates obtained.

Additionally, a comparative analysis of the other sensors used to detect Hg2+ is presented in Table S11. The nanosensor used in this study had a low detection limit and can be used for high-selectivity and high-sensitivity sensing of Hg2+ in real water environments.

In summary, the nanomaterials B, N-CDs, employing excellent optical properties, were prepared via a one-step hydrothermal synthesis using sucrose, boric acid, and melamine as raw materials for fluorescence and colorimetric dual-mode sensing of Hg2+. Static and dynamic quenching occurred simultaneously in the presence of Hg2+, enabling the sensor to be utilized as fluorescence sensors for high selectivity and sensitivity to detect Hg2+. Moreover, through controlling the colorimetric signal that controls the emergence of the TMB cation radical, B, N-CDs were designed for detecting Hg2+. The addition of Cys could inhibit the colorimetric signal, whereas Hg2+ restored the blue color because of the stronger interaction with Cys. The LOD in the fluorescent and colorimetric detection modes were 5.3 nM and 7.3 nM, respectively. Furthermore, B, N-CDs rendered effective Hg2+ monitoring from contaminated standard water samples, exhibiting satisfactory recovery rates ranging from 96% to 104%. The proposed method thus provides an applicable method for the facile dual-mode sensing of heavy metal ions using nanomaterials, delivering the significant potential for environmental monitoring and remediation in resolving the challenge of water pollution.

Qing Fu: Methodology, Data curation, Software, Writing original draft, Caicheng Long: Conceptualization, Writing-Review & Editing, Jiaoyan Huang: Writing-Review & Editing, Sicong Liu: Writing-Review & Editing, Taiping Qin: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing-Review & Editing, Peng Zhang: Investigation, Writing-Review & Editing, Bo Feng: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing original draft, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant no. 21777135, 51708475) and Hunan Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (grant no. 2019JJ40283, 2019JJ50596, 2018JJ3496), the Research Foundation of Education Bureau of Hunan Province (19A501, 18C0102), and Hunan 2011 Collaborative Innovation Center of Chemical Engineering & Technology with Environmental Benignity and Effective Resource Utilization.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2021.106882.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.
